description: Terraform / HCL invariants. Catches publicly-exposed ACLs, wide-open security groups, deletion-unprotected stateful resources, and inline secret strings. Apply to any Terraform repo. Extended with: terraform-public-access-block-disabled, terraform-rds-publicly-accessible, terraform-storage-unencrypted, terraform-iam-principal-wildcard, terraform-skip-final-snapshot, terraform-provisioner-exec, terraform-weak-tls-policy.

rules.//: S3 buckets with public ACLs are a frequent cause of data leaks. The correct pattern is private ACL + explicit bucket policy if exposure is genuinely required. Pattern covers HCL (`acl = "public-read"`) and Terraform JSON (`"acl": "public-read"`).
rules.message: S3 bucket configured with public ACL. Use private ACL plus an explicit bucket policy if exposure is genuinely required.

rules.//: 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on a security group is sometimes correct (public-facing LB) but is far more often a misconfig. Warn level so the author confirms intent. Pattern matches the CIDR anywhere in the array value, covering HCL and Terraform JSON.
rules.message: Security group rule open to 0.0.0.0/0. Confirm this is genuinely public-facing; otherwise scope to known CIDRs or a security-group source.

rules.//: RDS/Aurora deletion protection MUST be on for durable data stores. Catches `deletion_protection = false` (HCL) and `"deletion_protection": false` (Terraform JSON) literals. The negative lookbehind `(?<![a-zA-Z_])` anchors the match so that `enable_deletion_protection = false` (aws_lb attribute) is NOT matched — the `_` before `deletion_protection` disqualifies it. The affirmative case is not enforced; use Terraform Sentinel or OPA for presence-enforcement.
rules.message: deletion_protection = false on a Terraform resource. Stateful resources (RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB) must keep deletion protection enabled in production; set deletion_protection = true. Do not simply omit the field — the provider default is false for most resources.

rules.//: An aws_kms_key with prevent_destroy explicitly set to false can be removed via `terraform destroy` — devastating for data encrypted with that CMK. Detects the explicit `prevent_destroy = false` or `\"prevent_destroy\": false` literal near an aws_kms_key resource. Omission of prevent_destroy is not flagged (the linter cannot do full block-scope analysis); use Terraform Sentinel or OPA for presence-enforcement.
rules.message: prevent_destroy = false on a resource — KMS keys and other durable resources must not have prevent_destroy explicitly disabled. Set prevent_destroy = true in the lifecycle block. Do not simply remove the false line — omission defaults to false, leaving the resource destroyable.

rules.//: Hardcoded credentials in HCL or Terraform JSON. Detects `password = "<8+ chars>"` (HCL) and `"password": "<8+ chars>"` (JSON) literals, with common placeholder strings excluded.
rules.message: Credential assigned a string literal in Terraform — load from a variable, data source (aws_secretsmanager_secret_version), or environment. Rotate the value immediately if it was committed.

rules.//: Inline IAM policy with `Action: *` and `Resource: *` is the IAM equivalent of root. Warn-level because a service-linked role legitimately needs it occasionally. Pattern covers JSON-style (`"Action": "*"` or `"Action": ["*"]`), HCL jsonencode (`Action = "*"` or `Action = ["*"]`), and YAML-in-heredoc forms. A wildcard array `["*"]` is semantically identical to a scalar `"*"` in IAM. Two alternating branches match both field orderings (Action before Resource, and Resource before Action) because JSON/HCL object key order is not semantic. Scope limitation: the line-by-line linter matches this multi-line regex against each ADDED line; it fires when both wildcards appear in the forward window of the added line. For the case where only the second wildcard field is newly added (the other pre-exists in the file), the two single-field companion rules below (`terraform-iam-wildcard-action` / `terraform-iam-wildcard-resource`) catch it independently.
rules.message: IAM policy with Action="*" (or ["*"]) AND Resource="*" (or ["*"]) — scope at least one dimension (action set OR resource ARN pattern). Document the rationale if this is service-linked / break-glass.

rules.//: Companion to terraform-iam-wildcard-action-and-resource: catches a newly added `Action = "*"` line even when `Resource = "*"` is already present (pre-existing) in the same policy block — a case the multi-line rule misses because the pre-existing line is not in the added-lines window.
rules.message: IAM Action wildcard ("*") added — verify that Resource is also scoped (not "*"). A full Action=*/Resource=* grant is the IAM equivalent of root; document the rationale for any service-linked / break-glass role.

rules.//: Companion to terraform-iam-wildcard-action-and-resource: catches a newly added `Resource = "*"` line even when `Action = "*"` is already present (pre-existing) in the same policy block — a case the multi-line rule misses because the pre-existing line is not in the added-lines window.
rules.message: IAM Resource wildcard ("*") added — verify that Action is also scoped (not "*"). A full Action=*/Resource=* grant is the IAM equivalent of root; document the rationale for any service-linked / break-glass role.

rules.//: The aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block (and the account-level analog) is the last-line guardrail against accidental public exposure. Setting any of its four flags to false re-opens the door that the resource exists to close. Catches HCL (`block_public_acls = false`) and Terraform JSON (`"block_public_acls": false`) for all four attributes. Fix: keep all four true unless a bucket is deliberately, reviewably public — and then scope it with an explicit bucket policy, not by disabling the block.
rules.message: S3 public-access-block flag set to false — this re-opens the public-exposure guardrail. Keep block_public_acls / block_public_policy / ignore_public_acls / restrict_public_buckets all true; scope any intentional exposure via an explicit bucket policy instead.

rules.//: publicly_accessible = true on an aws_db_instance / aws_rds_cluster_instance assigns the database a public IP and (with a permissive SG) exposes it directly to the internet — a frequent breach vector. Covers HCL and Terraform JSON literals. Fix: set publicly_accessible = false and reach the DB through a private subnet / VPN / bastion.
rules.message: publicly_accessible = true on an RDS instance exposes the database to the public internet. Set it to false and reach the DB via a private subnet / VPN / bastion.

rules.//: Explicitly disabling encryption-at-rest (`storage_encrypted = false` on RDS, `encrypted = false` on aws_ebs_volume / aws_ebs_snapshot / default EBS encryption) ships unencrypted data to disk. The negative lookbehind `(?<![A-Za-z_])` anchors the attribute name so a longer attribute ending in `encrypted` is not misread; the listed names (storage_encrypted, encrypted) are the common ones. Covers HCL and Terraform JSON. Fix: set the flag to true (and supply a CMK via kms_key_id for customer-managed keys).
rules.message: Encryption-at-rest explicitly disabled (storage_encrypted/encrypted = false). Set it to true and supply a kms_key_id for a customer-managed key where required.

rules.//: A resource-based policy whose Principal (or its nested AWS key) is "*" grants access to every AWS account / anonymous caller — for an S3 bucket policy, SQS/SNS, or KMS key policy this is public access. Sometimes intentional (a deliberately public bucket) but far more often a misconfig, so WARN, matching the IAM-wildcard rules. Covers `"Principal": "*"`, `Principal = "*"`, and the nested `"AWS": "*"` / `"AWS": ["*"]` forms. Fix: name the specific account/role ARNs, or gate the wildcard with a tight Condition (aws:SourceArn, aws:PrincipalOrgID).
rules.message: IAM/resource policy Principal = "*" grants access to every account / anonymous caller (public). Name specific account or role ARNs, or constrain the wildcard with a tight Condition (aws:SourceArn, aws:PrincipalOrgID). Document the rationale if the resource is deliberately public.

rules.//: skip_final_snapshot = true means destroying an RDS/Aurora/DocumentDB instance leaves NO final backup — an irreversible data-loss footgun if the resource is ever torn down. Sometimes acceptable for throwaway/dev databases, so WARN. Fix: set skip_final_snapshot = false (and a final_snapshot_identifier) for any database holding data you cannot recreate.
rules.message: skip_final_snapshot = true — destroying this database takes no final backup (irreversible data loss). Set it to false with a final_snapshot_identifier for any database holding non-recreatable data.

rules.//: A local-exec / remote-exec provisioner runs arbitrary shell on `terraform apply` — outside the provider graph, with no state tracking, often invoking curl|sh or embedding credentials. It is the Terraform supply-chain / arbitrary-code sink. HashiCorp itself documents provisioners as a last resort. WARN: a legitimate bootstrap exists occasionally. Fix: prefer a provider resource, cloud-init/user_data, or a purpose-built tool; if a provisioner is unavoidable, keep the command argv-explicit and credential-free.
rules.message: local-exec/remote-exec provisioner runs arbitrary shell on apply (Terraform's arbitrary-code sink) — prefer a provider resource or cloud-init/user_data. If unavoidable, keep the command argv-explicit and free of inline credentials.

rules.//: Pinning minimum_protocol_version (CloudFront viewer cert, API Gateway domain, ELB security policy) to SSLv3 / TLSv1 / TLSv1.1 (or the CloudFront `TLSv1_2016` alias, which is TLS 1.0) negotiates protocols with known weaknesses. The closing quote in the pattern prevents matching the safe `TLSv1.2_2021` / `TLSv1.2` values. Fix: use TLSv1.2_2021 (CloudFront) or TLSv1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 where supported.
rules.message: minimum_protocol_version pinned to a weak policy (SSLv3/TLSv1/TLSv1.1, or the TLSv1_2016 alias = TLS 1.0). Use TLSv1.2_2021 (CloudFront) or TLSv1.2 minimum, TLS 1.3 where supported.
